nativeRange.habitat: Endemic to the island of Borneo, growing as a rheophyte on rocks and submerged wood along the banks and beds of fast-flowing rainforest streams and rivers; many populations are periodically emersed and resubmerged as water levels rise and fall

decorPreferences: lava rock, driftwood, or other porous hardscape to attach the rhizome to
good flow across the rhizome to mimic rheophyte stream conditions
shaded or moderately lit areas; tolerates being placed in foreground or midground
stable, established tank — do not introduce into newly cycled systems

goodWith: shrimp (Neocaridina and Caridina) which graze biofilm without harming the plant
other low-light epiphytes such as Anubias, Java fern and mosses
avoidWith: large herbivorous or rhizome-disturbing fish such as goldfish, silver dollars and large plecos
vigorous diggers (large cichlids) that may dislodge attached rhizomes
newly set up tanks with unstable parameters

summary: Bucephalandra is a slow-growing epiphytic aroid endemic to Borneo, treated in the hobby much like Anubias. Many named cultivars exist; the rhizome must be attached to rock or wood and never buried, and the plant is prone to a temporary 'buce melt' when first acclimating to submersed conditions.
careNotes: Bucephalandra is a genus of small flowering aroids (family Araceae) endemic to the island of Borneo, where they grow as rheophytes on rocks and wood in fast-flowing streams. In the aquarium they are treated very similarly to Anubias: the creeping rhizome is attached to hardscape (lava rock, driftwood, slate) using super glue gel, sewing thread, or fishing line, and is left exposed to the water column. Burying the rhizome in substrate is generally reported to cause it to rot. Reported water-parameter ranges across hobby sources cluster around roughly 22-28 °C and pH ~6.0-7.5 (Aquarium Co-Op gives 21-28 °C / pH 6-8; Aquariadise 21.5-28 °C / pH 6.0-7.5; AquariumBreeder 22-28 °C / pH 5-8). The 2HR Aquarist notes the genus has fewer stress/melt issues at cooler temperatures and may struggle at sustained discus temperatures (>27-28 °C). Lighting needs are flexible: low light is sufficient for slow survival growth, while medium light brings out the characteristic blue, purple, red and iridescent metallic sheens (a structural-color effect from leaf microstructure rather than pigment). High light without CO2 tends to trigger algae on the slow-growing leaves. CO2 is generally considered optional but produces visibly better growth and color. The plant absorbs nutrients from the water column, so substrate type is largely irrelevant and root tabs are not generally needed; light liquid fertilization is sufficient. Growth is very slow — commonly reported as one new leaf every 2-4 weeks in low-tech setups, slightly faster with CO2 and brighter light. Adult size depends strongly on the cultivar: creeping/dwarf forms such as 'Mini Phantom' and 'Wavy Green' stay roughly 5-10 cm, while taller cultivars such as 'Kedagang' and 'Godzilla' can reach 15-25 cm; leaf shapes range from rounded to lance-like and wavy. 'Buce melt' is widely reported: newly imported plants that were grown emersed shed their original leaves when first submerged, and existing leaves can melt back after sudden parameter changes (temperature, pH, TDS) or transport stress. As long as the rhizome stays firm and white/green inside, recovery from the rhizome is generally reported and new submersed-form leaves emerge over the following weeks. The 2HR Aquarist and other guides advise introducing Bucephalandra only into established, stable tanks. Many named hobby cultivars exist (e.g. 'Brownie', 'Velvet', 'Theia', 'Catherinae', 'Mini Boyan', 'Godzilla', 'Artemis', 'Kedagang', 'Black Pearl', 'Needle Leaf', 'Bukit Kelam', 'Wavy Green', 'Mini Phantom') — they are documented as separate entries; this genus-level record covers shared husbandry. Cross-source disagreement on pH was averaged toward the conservative 6.0-7.5 range; the wider 5-8 spread is recorded above for the reviewer.
breedingNotes: Standard hobby propagation is by rhizome division: once the plant has established and put out new leaves and roots, cut the rhizome at a natural bend or constriction with clean, sharp scissors, leaving each division with several leaves and visible roots, and reattach the new piece to a separate piece of hardscape. Sources recommend divisions of at least roughly 4 cm of rhizome. Sexual reproduction via the genus' characteristic spathe/spadix inflorescence is occasionally observed in emersed and paludarium setups but is not a practical hobby propagation method.

primary.name: Tropica
primary.notes: Primary source. The Tropica search landing URL supplied by the controller returns navigation only; this is the representative Bucephalandra pygmaea 'Wavy Green' product page used for Tropica's care ratings. Tropica describes Bucephalandra as rhizomatous epiphytes from Asia that grow on rocks/wood in streams and must not have the rhizome buried; rates the genus as Easy, low light demand, low CO2 demand, slow growth.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op
additional.notes: Cross-checked Borneo rheophyte origin, water parameters (70-82°F / 21-28°C, pH 6-8), low-to-medium lighting, optional CO2, foreground/midground/hardscape placement, propagation by rhizome cutting, exposed rhizome, iridescent coloration, suitability for nano tanks, and emersed-to-submersed 'buce melt'.

additional.name: 2HR Aquarist
additional.notes: Used (via search snippet; direct fetch returned 403) for the observation that Bucephalandra is more stress- and melt-resistant in cooler water, struggles somewhat above discus temperatures (~27-28 °C), benefits from good flow and CO2 for full coloration, and should only be introduced into stable, established tanks.

additional.name: Aquariadise
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters (21.5-28 °C, pH 6.0-7.5), low-to-medium lighting, slow growth (1 leaf every 2-4 weeks), structural-color iridescence, propagation by cutting rhizome sections with leaves and roots, requirement to attach to hardscape, never burying the rhizome.

additional.name: AquariumBreeder
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Araceae, rheophyte with creeping rhizome), Borneo endemism, water parameters (22-28 °C, pH 5-8, gH 5+ preferred), adult size range across cultivars (5-60 cm reported but 5-10 cm typical for most aquarium varieties), propagation by rhizome division of at least 4 cm, and warning that recovery from damage can take weeks to months.
